title: About the Maps
text:
This site turns GPX rides into route notes for BART-accessible cycling. Each route page starts from the recorded track points in the source GPX file, computes per-step distance, grade, bearing change, and elevation gain, then converts adjacent points into mapped route segments.
The maps are meant to help compare routes before a ride. They are not turn-by-turn navigation, and they inherit the usual limitations of GPS tracks, barometric or device elevation data, OpenStreetMap coverage, and public pavement-condition datasets.

title: About the Maps
About the Maps
text:
Hazards Map
The Hazards map colors each short route segment by ride type. The classification is based primarily on effective grade, with extra attention to sharp turns on descents.
Before classification, short segments are smoothed with a centered distance-weighted rolling grade. This reduces the effect of tiny GPS segments where a small elevation wobble can create an unrealistic grade spike.
The raw grade categories are:

Flat: everything not caught by the climb or descent thresholds.
Light Descent: grade at or below -4.0%.
Descent: grade at or below -7.9%, including sharp turns on descents in the simplified site profile.
Steep Descent: grade at or below -19.9%, including sharp turns on steep descents in the simplified site profile.
Climb: grade at or above 4.0%.
Steep Climb: grade at or above 7.9%.

A turn is treated as notable when the bearing change rounds above 19.9 degrees. In the published simplified profile, turn-on-descent cases are folded into the descent categories so the map stays readable.
The table under the map sums route distance by these categories. It is a distance summary, not a risk score. A short, awkward descent may matter more in practice than its mileage suggests.

title: About the Maps
About the Maps
text:
Street Quality Map
The Street Quality map combines route geometry with two outside data sources:

OpenStreetMap, used to identify nearby road names, road types, gravel, and cycleways.
MTC Vital Signs pavement-condition data, used where a route segment can be matched to a public roadway record.

For each route segment, the build searches a narrow corridor around the route, scores candidate street matches by overlap, bearing, distance, and name similarity, and keeps the best candidate. When no pavement-condition record can be matched, the segment is marked as unknown rather than left blank.
The detailed pavement labels are simplified on the map:

Great: Excellent or Very Good.
Good: Good or Fair.
Ok: At Risk.
Poor: Poor or Failed.
Gravel: route segments identified as gravel.
Cycleway: route segments identified as cycleways.
Roadway (Unknown) or Cycleway (Unknown): places where the route is known but pavement condition is not.

The road-quality score shown on route cards is the share of rated, non-gravel roadway miles that are Excellent, Very Good, Good, or Fair. Gravel and unknown cycleway miles are excluded from that denominator so mixed-surface routes are not automatically penalized for being intentionally off-road or separated from the street network.

title: About the Maps
text:
Direction Markers
Some routes overlap themselves. When the outbound and return portions share meaningful geometry, the map may show a Route Pass control so only one direction is highlighted at a time. Number markers and chevrons are added to help show route order and direction, especially on loopier routes.
